input.plan:
## Local sign-in into org-next via the auth app

The two apps already have everything you need; this is mostly an env-file + workflow change, not a code change. Two complementary local bypasses are already built into the apps:

### Option A — Mock mode (fastest; UI-only, no Cognito, no real backend)

The auth app supports `VITE_AUTH_MOCK_MODE=true` which makes [submitSignInFn](apps/auth/src/features/auth/server-functions.ts) accept **any email + any password**, skip Cognito, and write valid signed `authToken`/`refreshToken`/`idToken` cookies for org-next. Same for sign-up + confirm — codes are not validated. This is the “bypass the email code” path.

Caveat: the access token in those cookies is `mock-access-<uuid>`. The Rails `anedot_next` backend at `http://localhost:3000` will return 401 for that token, which org-next will turn back into `/login`. Use this option when you only need the UI flow or are mocking the backend.

Steps:

1. Create `apps/auth/.env.local` from `apps/auth/.env.template`. Keep:

   ```
   VITE_AUTH_MOCK_MODE=true
   AUTH_SESSION_SECRET=<>=32 chars, not the placeholder value>
   ORG_NEXT_SESSION_SECRET=3a76a3c0-5cc3-41a9-9258-5c9a82b94159
   AUTH_APPS={"org-next":{"sessionSecretEnv":"ORG_NEXT_SESSION_SECRET","allowedOrigins":["http://localhost:3210"],"cookieNames":{"auth":"authToken","refresh":"refreshToken","id":"idToken"},"cookieDomain":""}}
   ```

   Cognito vars can stay empty.

2. Create `apps/org-next/.env.local` from [apps/org-next/.env.template](apps/org-next/.env.template). Critical: `SESSION_SECRET` MUST equal `ORG_NEXT_SESSION_SECRET` byte-for-byte, and `VITE_AUTH_APP_URL=http://localhost:3208` must match the auth app's port.

3. Restart both dev servers (env changes do not hot-reload).

4. In the browser, hit `http://localhost:3210/login`. You'll be bounced to `http://localhost:3208/sign-in?...`. Enter any email + any password and submit. The auth app writes the three signed cookies for org-next and redirects back; you're now signed in. Special test emails: any email containing `mfa`, `newpass`, or `unconfirmed` triggers the corresponding mock challenge (see [server-functions.ts](apps/auth/src/features/auth/server-functions.ts) lines ~474–500); avoid those substrings for the simple path.

If you instead see `/error?reason=unknown_app`, the running auth process did not load `AUTH_APPS["org-next"]` — restart auth after the env edit. This is documented in [apps/auth/docs/app-integration.md](apps/auth/docs/app-integration.md).

### Option B — Test-seed cookies (real Cognito access token, real backend)

If Option A's mock token can't reach the real backend, skip the auth app entirely for sign-in and seed real cookies directly via the org-next test-only endpoint. The route is [apps/org-next/src/routes/test-seed.auth.ts](apps/org-next/src/routes/test-seed.auth.ts) and is gated by `E2E_TEST_SEED=true`.

1. Add `E2E_TEST_SEED=true` to `apps/org-next/.env.local`. (See the production warning in the [docs](apps/org-next/docs/README.md): never set this on a deployed env.)

2. Restart org-next.

3. Obtain a real Cognito access token for an admin user. Two easy ways:
   - One-off, never-expires sign-up: run a real `pnpm --filter auth dev` against the real Cognito user pool and sign up. To skip the emailed confirmation code, run AWS CLI as a pool admin:
     - `aws cognito-idp admin-confirm-sign-up --user-pool-id <pool> --username <email> --region us-west-2`
     - or pre-create a confirmed admin user with `aws cognito-idp admin-create-user … --message-action SUPPRESS` followed by `admin-set-user-password … --permanent`.
   - Or grab a still-valid `accessToken` value from any existing `authToken` cookie you already have.
4. Visit `http://localhost:3210/test-seed/auth?authToken=<paste-token>&idToken=<optional>&refreshToken=<optional>`. The route just returns nothing visible; cookies are now set. Navigate to `/` and you're signed in — server functions in [src/server/org-api.server.ts](apps/org-next/src/server/org-api.server.ts) will forward the real token to the Rails backend.

5. To clear: `http://localhost:3210/test-seed/auth?action=clear`.

### Decision guide

- Just want UI-level sign-in for org-next pages without a real backend → Option A.
- Need to actually call the Rails API at `localhost:3000` while signed in → Option B with a Cognito token from an admin-confirmed user.

### Notes / non-goals

- No code changes are required. Both bypasses already exist and are documented in [apps/auth/docs/app-integration.md](apps/auth/docs/app-integration.md) and [apps/org-next/docs/README.md](apps/org-next/docs/README.md).
- `E2E_TEST_SEED` and `VITE_AUTH_MOCK_MODE` must never be set in production.
- If you'd prefer a single "always-let-me-in as admin" button (e.g. a dev-only link on the auth sign-in screen that mints a real Cognito token via the Admin API), that is a small additional code change and we should treat it as a follow-up — happy to write that plan if you want it.

overview: Make auth, org-next, and the anedot_next Rails backend share a single ANEDOT_LOCAL_DEV flag so any login credentials work end-to-end in local dev, and add a root-of-auth-app integration guide for new consumer apps.
todos:

  - id: real-shape-jwt
    content: Have the auth app mint a real-shape HS256 JWT (instead of mock-access-<uuid>) signed with LOCAL_DEV_JWT_SECRET when ANEDOT_LOCAL_DEV=true, so the Rails backend can decode it.
  - id: anedot-next-decoder
    content: Update anedot_next CognitoJwtValidator + initializer to switch to HS256 + LOCAL_DEV_JWT_SECRET when ANEDOT_LOCAL_DEV=true and skip JWKS/issuer/client_id checks.

  - id: integration-doc
    content: Create apps/auth/INTEGRATING.md — a root-of-auth-app guide covering both the integration contract and the local dev quickstart for any new consumer app.
isProject: false

# Local development across auth, org-next, and anedot_next

## Goal

A new developer can clone all three repos, set one shared env var, and sign into org-next (or any new consumer app) at `http://localhost:3210` with **any** email + password — including reaching the real Rails API at `http://localhost:3000` — without ever touching Cognito or an emailed verification code. Production behavior is unchanged when the flag is unset.

The plan keeps the existing app-mode integration contract from [apps/auth/docs/app-integration.md](apps/auth/docs/app-integration.md) untouched; it only changes what *kind* of token the auth app mints in local dev and how the Rails backend validates it.

## The unified flag: `ANEDOT_LOCAL_DEV=true`

One name, three apps:

| App | Reads as | Behavior when `true` |
|---|---|---|
| `apps/auth` | `process.env.ANEDOT_LOCAL_DEV` | Skip Cognito on sign-in/up/confirm; mint a real-shape HS256 JWT for the access token; any email+password accepted. |
| `apps/org-next` | `process.env.ANEDOT_LOCAL_DEV` (also exposed via `import.meta.env` if we want a banner) | No functional change — already passes the cookie's bearer through. Optional: render a small "LOCAL DEV" banner. |
| `anedot_next` | `ENV["ANEDOT_LOCAL_DEV"]` | `CognitoJwtValidator` decodes HS256 with `LOCAL_DEV_JWT_SECRET` and skips JWKS / `iss` / `client_id` checks; user lookup-or-create still keys on `sub`/`email`. |

Plus one shared HS256 secret across the auth app and the Rails backend:

```
LOCAL_DEV_JWT_SECRET=<at least 32 chars, identical on auth + anedot_next>
```

Production never sets either variable, so all the new code paths are dead.

## Token shape (the only new contract)

When `ANEDOT_LOCAL_DEV=true`, the auth app issues a JWT in this shape (instead of `mock-access-<uuid>`):

```json
{
  "alg": "HS256",
  "typ": "JWT"
}
```

Payload:

```json
{
  "iss": "anedot-local-dev",
  "sub": "local-dev|<email>",
  "email": "<whatever the user typed>",
  "given_name": "Local",
  "family_name": "Dev",
  "token_use": "access",
  "client_id": "anedot-local-dev",
  "iat": <unix>,
  "exp": <unix + 1h>,
  "jti": "<uuid>"
}
```

- HMAC-SHA256 signed with `LOCAL_DEV_JWT_SECRET`.
- `sub` is deterministic per email so re-signing in lands on the same Rails `User` row.
- All claim names match what `CognitoJwtValidator` already checks for in production (`sub`, `email`, `token_use=="access"`, `client_id`), so the validator branches on `ANEDOT_LOCAL_DEV` and reuses the same downstream code.

## Per-app changes

### `apps/auth`

Files to touch:

- [apps/auth/src/lib/env.ts](apps/auth/src/lib/env.ts):
  - Add `isLocalDev()` reading `process.env.ANEDOT_LOCAL_DEV === "true"`.
  - Make `isMockAuthEnabled()` return `isLocalDev() || process.env.VITE_AUTH_MOCK_MODE === "true"` for backward compat (and console.warn the legacy name).
  - Add `getLocalDevJwtSecret()` that throws if `isLocalDev()` and the secret is missing or <32 chars (mirrors `getSessionSecret`).
- New file `apps/auth/src/lib/local-dev-jwt.ts` exporting `signLocalDevAccessToken({ email })`. Reuse the base64url + HMAC patterns already in [bootstrap-jwt.ts](apps/auth/src/lib/bootstrap-jwt.ts).
- [apps/auth/src/features/auth/server-functions.ts](apps/auth/src/features/auth/server-functions.ts):
  - In `submitSignInFn` / `submitConfirmSignUpFn` / `submitChallengeFn`'s mock branches that currently call `setMockAuthCookie` or `redirectToAppReturn`, swap the synthetic `mock-access-<uuid>` for a call into `writeAppTokenCookies(app, { accessToken: signLocalDevAccessToken({ email }), idToken: signLocalDevAccessToken({ email, token_use: "id" }), refreshToken: undefined })` so the cookies the consumer reads now contain a real-shape JWT.
  - Identical change to the OAuth (non-app) mock branch (`setMockAuthCookie`) so old consumers also benefit.
- [apps/auth/.env.template](apps/auth/.env.template): replace `VITE_AUTH_MOCK_MODE=true` with `ANEDOT_LOCAL_DEV=true` and add `LOCAL_DEV_JWT_SECRET=replace-with-at-least-32-characters` next to `AUTH_SESSION_SECRET`. Leave `VITE_AUTH_MOCK_MODE` out (deprecated; fallback still works).
- [apps/auth/README.md](apps/auth/README.md): replace the "Local development" section with one paragraph that points at the new `INTEGRATING.md` for a full step-by-step.
- Tests: rename `VITE_AUTH_MOCK_MODE` → `ANEDOT_LOCAL_DEV` in [apps/auth/.env.test](apps/auth/.env.test) and [apps/auth/playwright.config.ts](apps/auth/playwright.config.ts). No production semantics change.

Touchstone: existing tests in [apps/auth/src/lib/env.test.ts](apps/auth/src/lib/env.test.ts) and `app-integration.org-next.test.ts` should still pass after the `isMockAuthEnabled()` shim, with one new test for `signLocalDevAccessToken`.

### `apps/org-next`

Files to touch (small, mostly env):

- [apps/org-next/.env.template](apps/org-next/.env.template): add `ANEDOT_LOCAL_DEV=true` (commented-out by default with a one-liner explanation). No functional code path needs it today — it exists so the variable is greppable and consistent with the other apps.
- [apps/org-next/src/env.ts](apps/org-next/src/env.ts) (file not yet read; verify): add an optional `ANEDOT_LOCAL_DEV: z.coerce.boolean().default(false)` so we can later light up a "LOCAL DEV" banner without further plumbing.
- [apps/org-next/docs/README.md](apps/org-next/docs/README.md): update the "Auth flow (cross-app)" section to mention the unified flag and link to the new auth-app integration doc.

Explicitly **out of scope** for this plan: any change to org-next's `org-api.server.ts` token forwarding — it already does exactly the right thing by passing the cookie's bearer through.

### `anedot_next` (Rails)

Files to touch:

- [anedot_next/config/initializers/cognito.rb](anedot_next/config/initializers/cognito.rb): extend the `Rails.application.config.cognito` hash with two more keys:

  ```ruby
  local_dev: ENV["ANEDOT_LOCAL_DEV"] == "true",
  local_dev_jwt_secret: ENV["LOCAL_DEV_JWT_SECRET"]
  ```

- [anedot_next/app/services/cognito_jwt_validator.rb](anedot_next/app/services/cognito_jwt_validator.rb): at the top of `#decode`, branch on `@config[:local_dev]`:

  ```ruby
  return decode_local_dev if @config[:local_dev]
  ```

## End-to-end local dev workflow (what a developer does)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Dev as Developer
    participant Browser
    participant OrgNext as org-next:3210
    participant Auth as auth:3208
    participant Rails as anedot_next:3000

    Dev->>OrgNext: pnpm --filter org-next dev
    Dev->>Auth: pnpm --filter auth dev
    Dev->>Rails: bin/rails server (with ANEDOT_LOCAL_DEV=true)
    Browser->>OrgNext: GET /login
    OrgNext-->>Browser: 302 -> auth/authorize?app=org-next
    Browser->>Auth: GET /authorize
    Auth-->>Browser: 302 -> /sign-in
    Browser->>Auth: POST /sign-in (any email + password)
    Auth->>Auth: signLocalDevAccessToken(email)
    Auth-->>Browser: Set-Cookie authToken=<HS256 JWT>; 302 -> org-next/
    Browser->>OrgNext: GET / with authToken cookie
    OrgNext->>Rails: GET /users/me Authorization: Bearer <JWT>
    Rails->>Rails: CognitoJwtValidator (local_dev branch)
    Rails-->>OrgNext: 200 user JSON
    OrgNext-->>Browser: render
```

The developer's checklist:

1. `apps/auth/.env.local` ← copy `.env.template`, set `ANEDOT_LOCAL_DEV=true`, `AUTH_SESSION_SECRET`, `LOCAL_DEV_JWT_SECRET`, `<APP>_SESSION_SECRET`, `AUTH_APPS`.
2. `apps/<your-app>/.env.local` ← `SESSION_SECRET` matching `<APP>_SESSION_SECRET`, `VITE_AUTH_APP_URL=http://localhost:3208`, `ANEDOT_LOCAL_DEV=true`.
3. `anedot_next/.env.development.local` ← `ANEDOT_LOCAL_DEV=true`, `LOCAL_DEV_JWT_SECRET=<same as auth>`.
4. Restart all three dev servers.
5. Hit `http://localhost:3210/login`, type any email/password, you're in.

## The new integration doc: `apps/auth/INTEGRATING.md`

A single root-of-auth-app file that any developer onboarding a new app can read top-to-bottom. Outline:

```
# Integrating a new app with the Anedot Auth app

## TL;DR
- 5-minute checklist
- Link to docs/app-integration.md for the formal contract

## 1. Pick a slug, share a secret
- AUTH_APPS entry shape (copy-paste with placeholders)
- Generate <APP>_SESSION_SECRET, paste into both auth env and your app env
- cookieDomain="" for localhost, ".anedot.com" in prod

## 2. Wire your app's login + logout
- buildAuthAppLoginUrl pattern (link to org-next's src/server/auth-state.ts as the reference impl)
- Sign auth_state with your SESSION_SECRET
- Read the three cookies via TanStack useSession with the same names + secret
- Forward authToken cookie value as Authorization: Bearer to your backend

## 3. Local development with `ANEDOT_LOCAL_DEV=true`
- The unified flag, the three apps that read it, the shared LOCAL_DEV_JWT_SECRET
- Mermaid diagram (same as above)
- Step-by-step env files for auth + your app + anedot_next
- "Any email + any password works" guarantee
- Sign-up / verification code paths also accept any input

## 4. Production deployment
- Drop ANEDOT_LOCAL_DEV; set Cognito creds + cookieDomain=".anedot.com"; share AUTH_APPS / SESSION_SECRET via your secret manager
- Reference the env-var matrix in docs/app-integration.md

## 5. Reference: org-next as the canonical example
- src/routes/login.tsx, src/routes/-auth.ts, src/server/auth-state.ts
- src/server/session.server.ts shows the cookie reader pattern
- src/server/org-api.server.ts shows bearer forwarding
- apps/org-next/docs/README.md "Auth flow (cross-app)" for the integration commentary
```

The doc deliberately does **not** repeat the formal contract from `docs/app-integration.md`; it links to it. It is written as a checklist a developer can execute, with concrete file paths from the org-next reference implementation.

## Out of scope (call out so it doesn't creep in)

- The Playwright `/test-seed/auth` route and `E2E_TEST_SEED` flag stay exactly as they are — separate machinery for tests.
- No "auto sign-in as admin" button on the auth screen. The local-dev flow still goes through the form once; this matches the production UX a developer is debugging.

class CognitoJwtValidatorLocalDevTest < ActiveSupport::TestCase
  LOCAL_DEV_SECRET = "local-dev-jwt-secret-at-least-32-characters-long"

  setup do
    @original_config = Rails.application.config.cognito
    Rails.application.config.cognito = {
      region: "us-east-1",
      user_pool_id: nil,
      client_id: nil,
      local_dev: true,
      local_dev_jwt_secret: LOCAL_DEV_SECRET
    }.freeze
  end

  teardown do
    Rails.application.config.cognito = @original_config
  end

  test "decodes an HS256 access token signed with the local dev secret" do
    token = sign_local_dev_token(email: "user@example.com", sub: "local-dev|user@example.com")
    payload = CognitoJwtValidator.new(token).decode
    assert_equal "user@example.com", payload["email"]
    assert_equal "local-dev|user@example.com", payload["sub"]
    assert_equal "access", payload["token_use"]
  end

  test "skips JWKS / issuer / client_id checks in local dev" do
    token = sign_local_dev_token(
      email: "user@example.com",
      sub: "local-dev|user@example.com",
      iss: "anedot-local-dev",
      client_id: "anedot-local-dev"
    )
    payload = CognitoJwtValidator.new(token).decode
    assert_equal "anedot-local-dev", payload["iss"]
    assert_equal "anedot-local-dev", payload["client_id"]
  end

  test "rejects an id token in local dev (wrong token_use)" do
    token = sign_local_dev_token(email: "user@example.com", token_use: "id")
    err = assert_raises(CognitoJwtValidator::InvalidToken) do
      CognitoJwtValidator.new(token).decode
    end
    assert_equal "Expected access token", err.message
  end

  test "rejects an HS256 token signed with a wrong secret in local dev" do
    payload = base_payload(email: "user@example.com")
    token = JWT.encode(payload, "different-secret-at-least-32-characters-long", "HS256")
    assert_raises(CognitoJwtValidator::InvalidToken) do
      CognitoJwtValidator.new(token).decode
    end
  end

  test "rejects when local_dev_jwt_secret is missing" do
    Rails.application.config.cognito = {
      region: "us-east-1",
      user_pool_id: nil,
      client_id: nil,
      local_dev: true,
      local_dev_jwt_secret: nil
    }.freeze

    token = sign_local_dev_token(email: "user@example.com")
    err = assert_raises(CognitoJwtValidator::InvalidToken) do
      CognitoJwtValidator.new(token).decode
    end
    assert_equal "Local dev JWT secret not configured", err.message
  end

  test "rejects an expired local dev token" do
    token = sign_local_dev_token(email: "user@example.com", exp: 60.minutes.ago.to_i)
    assert_raises(CognitoJwtValidator::InvalidToken) do
      CognitoJwtValidator.new(token).decode
    end
  end

  private

  def sign_local_dev_token(email:, sub: "local-dev|test", token_use: "access", iss: "anedot-local-dev", client_id: "anedot-local-dev", exp: nil)
    payload = base_payload(
      email: email,
      sub: sub,
      token_use: token_use,
      iss: iss,
      client_id: client_id,
      exp: exp
    )
    JWT.encode(payload, LOCAL_DEV_SECRET, "HS256")
  end

  def base_payload(email:, sub: "local-dev|test", token_use: "access", iss: "anedot-local-dev", client_id: "anedot-local-dev", exp: nil)
    {
      iss: iss,
      sub: sub,
      email: email,
      token_use: token_use,
      client_id: client_id,
      iat: Time.current.to_i,
      exp: exp || 60.minutes.from_now.to_i
    }
  end
end

input.contents:
# Anedot Auth App

Standalone auth portal for Anedot client applications.

## Local development

Local development uses the unified `ANEDOT_LOCAL_DEV=true` flag (the same
flag set on the consuming app and on the `anedot_next` Rails backend).
With it set, the auth app accepts any sign-in credentials and mints a
Cognito-shaped HS256 JWT signed with `LOCAL_DEV_JWT_SECRET` so the Rails
backend can decode it.

Run:

```
pnpm --filter auth dev
```

Open:

```
http://localhost:3208/authorize?app=org-next&...
```

For the full step-by-step on integrating a new app — including the local
dev quickstart that ties auth, your app, and `anedot_next` together — see
[INTEGRATING.md](./INTEGRATING.md).

The legacy `VITE_AUTH_MOCK_MODE=true` flag still works (it's aliased to
`ANEDOT_LOCAL_DEV`) but the auth app will log a deprecation warning. Move
to `ANEDOT_LOCAL_DEV` so the same env var is meaningful across all three
apps.

## Production / staging

Set `ANEDOT_LOCAL_DEV=false` (or leave it unset) and provide real Cognito
env values plus `AUTH_ALLOWED_URIS_BY_CLIENT_ID` (JSON map of `client_id`
-> allowed redirect/logout URIs) to exercise real redirect orchestration
and higher-environment auth validation.

## Environment variables

### Always required

- `AUTH_SESSION_SECRET` — at least 32 characters; used to sign/encrypt
  auth session data. Startup fails fast when missing, insecure, or short.
- `AUTH_APPS` — JSON registry of integrated apps. See
  [docs/app-integration.md](./docs/app-integration.md) for the shape.
- `AUTH_ALLOWED_URIS_BY_CLIENT_ID` — JSON object mapping each `client_id`
  to its allowed redirect/logout URIs (for the OAuth flow).

### Local dev only (when `ANEDOT_LOCAL_DEV=true`)

- `LOCAL_DEV_JWT_SECRET` — at least 32 characters; identical to the
  value the `anedot_next` Rails backend reads as `LOCAL_DEV_JWT_SECRET`.

### Cognito-backed mode (`ANEDOT_LOCAL_DEV` unset/false)

- `COGNITO_DOMAIN` — Cognito hosted UI domain URL.
- `COGNITO_CLIENT_ID` — Cognito app client ID used by AnedotAuth.
- `COGNITO_CLIENT_SECRET` — Cognito app client secret (if configured).
- `COGNITO_USER_POOL_ID` — Cognito user pool ID.
- `COGNITO_REGION` — AWS region for the user pool (for example `us-west-2`).

## End-to-end tests

Playwright starts its own preview server on **port 3208** locally
(**3218** in CI) (`pnpm e2e`). Do not run another process on that port at
the same time, or the run will fail to bind.

input.contents:
# Integrating a new app with the Anedot Auth app

This is the developer-facing checklist for wiring a new internal Anedot
app into the auth app. It covers the integration contract and the local
development quickstart that ties auth, your app, and the `anedot_next`
Rails backend together.

For the formal protocol (cookie envelope, authorize / logout URL
contract, refresh endpoint, return_to / auth_state semantics), read
[docs/app-integration.md](./docs/app-integration.md). This file is the
how-to; that file is the spec.

## TL;DR

1. Pick a slug for your app (e.g. `my-app`) and add an entry to the
   `AUTH_APPS` JSON registry on the auth app.
2. Generate a 32+ char `SESSION_SECRET` and put the same value on **both**
   sides (auth app via the `sessionSecretEnv` lookup, your app via its own
   `SESSION_SECRET`).
3. Wire `/login` in your app to redirect to
   `<auth-url>/authorize?app=<slug>&return_to=<rel>&auth_state=<signed>`.
4. Read the three `httpOnly` cookies (`authToken`, `refreshToken`,
   `idToken`) via TanStack `useSession` with the same names + secret.
5. Forward the `authToken` cookie value as `Authorization: Bearer ...`
   to your backend.
6. **For local dev**, set `ANEDOT_LOCAL_DEV=true` and a shared
   `LOCAL_DEV_JWT_SECRET` on auth, your app, and `anedot_next`.

The reference implementation is `apps/org-next` — every link below points
at a concrete file you can copy from.

## 1. Pick a slug, share a secret

Add an entry to the auth app's `AUTH_APPS` env var (JSON keyed by slug):

```jsonc
{
  "my-app": {
    "sessionSecretEnv": "MY_APP_SESSION_SECRET",
    "allowedOrigins": ["http://localhost:3220", "https://my-app.anedot.com"],
    "cookieNames": {
      "auth": "authToken",
      "refresh": "refreshToken",
      "id": "idToken"
    },
    "cookieDomain": "",
    "returnToPolicy": "relative-only"
  }
}
```

Then in the auth app's env (`apps/auth/.env.local` for local dev, secret
manager for higher envs):

```
MY_APP_SESSION_SECRET=<32+ chars>
```

And in your app's env (`apps/<your-app>/.env.local`):

```
SESSION_SECRET=<same 32+ chars as above>
```

`cookieDomain=""` makes cookies host-scoped — the right choice for
`localhost:<port>` development. In production, set it to `.anedot.com`
(or whatever the parent domain is) so the cookies traverse subdomains.

The full `AUTH_APPS` schema, including `returnToPolicy: { allowedOrigins: [...] }`
and `cognitoClientId` overrides, is in
[docs/app-integration.md](./docs/app-integration.md).

## 2. Wire login + logout in your app

The reference is `apps/org-next/src/routes/-auth.ts` plus the helpers it
calls. The pieces you need:

### Login (browser-redirect to the auth app)

Build a signed authorize URL on the **server** (so your `SESSION_SECRET`
never crosses to the client), then `window.location.assign(url)`:

- [`apps/org-next/src/server/auth-app-url.server.ts`](../org-next/src/server/auth-app-url.server.ts)
  — calls `buildLoginUrl(env.VITE_AUTH_APP_URL, env.SESSION_SECRET, returnTo)`.
- [`apps/org-next/src/server/auth-state.ts`](../org-next/src/server/auth-state.ts)
  — pure URL-building + auth_state HMAC signing logic. Unit-tested in
  `auth-state.test.ts`.
- [`apps/org-next/src/routes/-auth.ts`](../org-next/src/routes/-auth.ts)
  — exposes `initiateLogin(returnTo)` to call from a click handler.

The result is a URL of the form:

```
${VITE_AUTH_APP_URL}/authorize?app=<slug>&return_to=<rel>&auth_state=<signed>
```

`auth_state` is HMAC-SHA256 over a small JSON envelope (`slug`, `returnTo`,
`nonce`, `iat`) signed with **your** `SESSION_SECRET`. The auth app
verifies it with the same secret resolved through the registry.

### Logout

Mirror the same idea — a server function that returns a URL, plus a
client helper that browser-redirects. See
[`apps/org-next/src/routes/-auth.ts`](../org-next/src/routes/-auth.ts)'s
`performLogout` / `initiateLogout`.

URL shape:

```
${VITE_AUTH_APP_URL}/logout?app=<slug>&return_to=<rel>
```

The auth app revokes the Cognito session (best effort), clears the three
cookies, and redirects back.

## 3. Read the cookies

Once the auth app finishes sign-in, three signed `httpOnly` cookies
appear on your app's host:

| Cookie | Payload | Lifetime | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| `authToken` | `{ authToken }` | ~access token TTL | Forward to your backend as `Authorization: Bearer <token>`. |
| `idToken` | `{ idToken }` | ~access token TTL | For client-side identity surface only. |
| `refreshToken` | `{ refreshToken }` | ~30 days | Read only by the auth app. |

Read them with TanStack `useSession`. The cookie **names** and the
**secret** must match the registry entry exactly. Reference impl:
[`apps/org-next/src/server/session.server.ts`](../org-next/src/server/session.server.ts).

```ts
import { useSession } from "@tanstack/react-start/server";

export const useAuthTokenSession = createServerOnlyFn(() => {
  return useSession<{ authToken?: string }>({
    password: env.SESSION_SECRET,
    name: "authToken",
    cookie: { httpOnly: true, sameSite: "lax", secure: isSecure, maxAge: 3500, path: "/" },
  });
});
```

Forward the bearer to your backend (reference:
[`apps/org-next/src/server/org-api.server.ts`](../org-next/src/server/org-api.server.ts)):

```ts
const accessToken = (await useAuthTokenSession()).data.authToken;
fetch(url, { headers: { Authorization: `Bearer ${accessToken}` } });
```

Treat a missing cookie as `401` and redirect to `/login`. Do not invent a
fallback token.

## 4. Local development with `ANEDOT_LOCAL_DEV=true`

The auth app, your app, and `anedot_next` all read the same flag. When
set, sign-in accepts **any email + any password**, the auth app issues a
real-shape Cognito-style HS256 JWT, and the Rails backend decodes it
without going through Cognito JWKS.

### Dataflow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Browser
    participant App as your-app:<port>
    participant Auth as auth:3208
    participant Rails as anedot_next:3000

    Browser->>App: GET /login
    App-->>Browser: 302 -> auth/authorize?app=<slug>&auth_state=...
    Browser->>Auth: GET /authorize
    Auth-->>Browser: 302 -> /sign-in
    Browser->>Auth: POST /sign-in (any email + password)
    Auth->>Auth: signLocalDevAccessToken(email)
    Auth-->>Browser: Set-Cookie authToken=<HS256 JWT>; 302 -> your-app/
    Browser->>App: GET / with authToken cookie
    App->>Rails: GET /api Authorization: Bearer <JWT>
    Rails->>Rails: CognitoJwtValidator (local_dev branch)
    Rails-->>App: 200
    App-->>Browser: render
```

### Setup

Pick a single 32+ char value for `LOCAL_DEV_JWT_SECRET` and paste it
into all three env files. Also pick a 32+ char `SESSION_SECRET` for your
app and use the same value on both sides.

#### `apps/auth/.env.local`

```
ANEDOT_LOCAL_DEV=true
LOCAL_DEV_JWT_SECRET=<32+ chars, identical to anedot_next>
AUTH_SESSION_SECRET=<32+ chars>

# Add your app to the registry
MY_APP_SESSION_SECRET=<32+ chars, identical to your app's SESSION_SECRET>
AUTH_APPS={"my-app":{"sessionSecretEnv":"MY_APP_SESSION_SECRET","allowedOrigins":["http://localhost:3220"],"cookieNames":{"auth":"authToken","refresh":"refreshToken","id":"idToken"},"cookieDomain":""}}

# Cognito vars can stay empty in local dev
COGNITO_DOMAIN=
COGNITO_CLIENT_ID=
COGNITO_CLIENT_SECRET=
COGNITO_USER_POOL_ID=
COGNITO_REGION=us-west-2
```

#### `apps/<your-app>/.env.local`

```
ANEDOT_API_URL_BASE=http://localhost:3000
SESSION_SECRET=<same as MY_APP_SESSION_SECRET>
ANEDOT_LOCAL_DEV=true
VITE_AUTH_APP_URL=http://localhost:3208
```

#### `anedot_next/.env` (or `.env.development.local`)

```
ANEDOT_LOCAL_DEV=true
LOCAL_DEV_JWT_SECRET=<same value as in apps/auth/.env.local>
```

### Run all three

```
# terminal 1
pnpm --filter auth dev          # http://localhost:3208

# terminal 2
pnpm --filter <your-app> dev    # http://localhost:<your-port>

# terminal 3 (anedot_next)
bin/rails server                 # http://localhost:3000
```

Open `http://localhost:<your-port>/login`, type any email + any password,
submit. You're now signed in and authenticated against the real Rails
backend, which auto-creates a `User` row keyed on the JWT's `sub` (a
deterministic `local-dev|<email>` value) on first request.

### What the local-dev JWT looks like

```json
{
  "iss": "anedot-local-dev",
  "sub": "local-dev|<email>",
  "email": "<typed email>",
  "email_verified": true,
  "given_name": "Local",
  "family_name": "Dev",
  "token_use": "access",
  "client_id": "anedot-local-dev",
  "iat": <unix>,
  "exp": <unix + 3600>,
  "jti": "<uuid>"
}
```

HS256, signed with `LOCAL_DEV_JWT_SECRET`. Source:
[`apps/auth/src/lib/local-dev-jwt.ts`](./src/lib/local-dev-jwt.ts).

The Rails branch that decodes it lives in
[`anedot_next/app/services/cognito_jwt_validator.rb`](../../../anedot_next/app/services/cognito_jwt_validator.rb)
under `decode_local_dev`.

## 5. Production deployment

Drop `ANEDOT_LOCAL_DEV` and `LOCAL_DEV_JWT_SECRET` entirely. Set:

- `COGNITO_DOMAIN`, `COGNITO_CLIENT_ID`, `COGNITO_CLIENT_SECRET`,
  `COGNITO_USER_POOL_ID`, `COGNITO_REGION` on the auth app.
- `cookieDomain: ".anedot.com"` (or your parent domain) on every
  `AUTH_APPS` entry.
- `AUTH_COOKIE_DOMAIN` on the auth app to match.
- `AUTH_APPS`, the `<APP>_SESSION_SECRET` env vars, and your app's
  `SESSION_SECRET` via your secret manager.

Production never reads `ANEDOT_LOCAL_DEV` or `LOCAL_DEV_JWT_SECRET`, so
the local-dev code paths in both `apps/auth` and `anedot_next` stay
inert.

## 6. Reference: `org-next` as the canonical example

| Concern | File |
|---|---|
| `/login` route + redirect helper | [`apps/org-next/src/routes/login.tsx`](../org-next/src/routes/login.tsx), [`apps/org-next/src/routes/-auth.ts`](../org-next/src/routes/-auth.ts) |
| Build authorize / logout URLs | [`apps/org-next/src/server/auth-app-url.server.ts`](../org-next/src/server/auth-app-url.server.ts) |
| Pure URL + auth_state signing | [`apps/org-next/src/server/auth-state.ts`](../org-next/src/server/auth-state.ts) |
| Cookie readers | [`apps/org-next/src/server/session.server.ts`](../org-next/src/server/session.server.ts) |
| Bearer forwarding to backend | [`apps/org-next/src/server/org-api.server.ts`](../org-next/src/server/org-api.server.ts) |
| Bootstrap loader (gate + tenant pick) | [`apps/org-next/src/server/app-bootstrap.ts`](../org-next/src/server/app-bootstrap.ts) |
| Architecture commentary | [`apps/org-next/docs/README.md`](../org-next/docs/README.md) §"Auth flow (cross-app)" |

## Troubleshooting

- **`/error?reason=unknown_app`** on the auth app — the running auth
  process did not load `AUTH_APPS["<your slug>"]`. Restart the auth dev
  server after editing `.env.local`; env files don't hot-reload.
- **Sign-in redirects you back to `/login`** — your app's `SESSION_SECRET`
  doesn't match the auth app's `<APP>_SESSION_SECRET`. The cookies are
  set but not decodable. They must be byte-identical.
- **Backend returns `401` after sign-in (Rails)** — either
  `ANEDOT_LOCAL_DEV` isn't set on `anedot_next`, or
  `LOCAL_DEV_JWT_SECRET` doesn't match the auth app. The auth app's mint
  and the Rails decode use the same secret.
- **`Missing LOCAL_DEV_JWT_SECRET` thrown by the auth app** — the flag
  is on but the secret isn't set. Add it to `apps/auth/.env.local`.
- **Old sessions leak between modes** — clear cookies for `localhost:3208`
  and your app's host after toggling `ANEDOT_LOCAL_DEV` so the browser
  doesn't ship a token signed under a different scheme.

Quick answer: any cryptographically-random ≥32 char string works — both sides just need to hold byte-identical bytes. The easy command:

```bash
openssl rand -hex 32
```

That gives you 64 hex chars (32 random bytes) with no special characters to escape in `.env` files. Run it once, paste the same value into both places:

```bash
# generate once
SECRET=$(openssl rand -hex 32)

# auth side
echo "LOCAL_DEV_JWT_SECRET=$SECRET" >> apps/auth/.env.local

# anedot_next side
echo "LOCAL_DEV_JWT_SECRET=\"$SECRET\"" >> ../anedot_next/.env
```

If `openssl` isn't on PATH, the equivalents:

```bash
node -e 'console.log(require("crypto").randomBytes(32).toString("hex"))'
python3 -c 'import secrets; print(secrets.token_hex(32))'
ruby -rsecurerandom -e 'puts SecureRandom.hex(32)'
```

A few rules so it doesn't bite you:

- **Identical bytes on both sides.** The auth app signs with HS256; Rails verifies with the same key. One off-by-one whitespace and Rails returns 401. Quote it in `.env` if it might end up with shell-special characters (the `"` style in `anedot_next/.env` already does this).
- **Don't reuse `AUTH_SESSION_SECRET`, `<APP>_SESSION_SECRET`, or `SESSION_SECRET` for it.** They serve different roles (cookie sealing vs. JWT signing), and mixing them couples unrelated concerns. Generate a fresh value.
- **Don't commit it.** `apps/auth/.env.local` and `anedot_next/.env` are both gitignored — keep it that way. The committed `.env.template` / `.env` placeholders are intentionally non-secret so nobody copies them by accident.
- **Local dev only.** It must be unset in staging/production; the Rails validator only consults it when `ANEDOT_LOCAL_DEV=true`.
- **Length.** The auth-side `getLocalDevJwtSecret()` enforces ≥32 chars (`openssl rand -hex 32` produces 64, well above that).

You *can* commit a **template placeholder**, but not the actual shared secret.

Even if it’s random, once it’s committed it’s no longer secret. Anyone with repo access can mint a valid local-dev JWT that `anedot_next` would accept whenever `ANEDOT_LOCAL_DEV=true`. That’s lower risk than prod, but still not worth normalizing. Keep real values in ignored env files.

It should **not** encode an email. The secret is just the signing key. The email goes in the JWT payload at sign-in time:

```json
{
  "sub": "local-dev|dev@example.com",
  "email": "dev@example.com",
  "token_use": "access"
}
```

The right pattern is:

```env
# committed template
LOCAL_DEV_JWT_SECRET=replace-with-output-of-openssl-rand-hex-32
```

Then each dev runs:

```bash
openssl rand -hex 32
```

and pastes that same generated value into their local `apps/auth/.env.local` and `../anedot_next/.env`.

So: random secret, no email, never committed as a real value.

They are **not** used for the same thing, and in `org-next` specifically there should only be `SESSION_SECRET`, not `AUTH_SESSION_SECRET`.

What each secret does:

- `AUTH_SESSION_SECRET` lives in `apps/auth`. It seals the auth app’s **own internal session cookies**: transaction state, mock auth state, OAuth/bootstrap state. It is auth-app-private.
- `SESSION_SECRET` lives in `apps/org-next`. It seals org-next’s cookies and signs/verifies `auth_state`. The auth app also needs the same value under `ORG_NEXT_SESSION_SECRET` because it writes cookies that org-next later reads.
- `LOCAL_DEV_JWT_SECRET` is shared only between `apps/auth` and `anedot_next`. It signs/verifies the local-dev bearer JWT that Rails accepts when `ANEDOT_LOCAL_DEV=true`.

So no, don’t merge them. They are separate trust boundaries:

```text
AUTH_SESSION_SECRET
auth app internal cookies only

SESSION_SECRET / ORG_NEXT_SESSION_SECRET
auth app <-> org-next cookie envelope + auth_state

LOCAL_DEV_JWT_SECRET
auth app <-> anedot_next local-dev bearer JWT
```

But one thing from my doc wording is wrong: `LOCAL_DEV_JWT_SECRET` does **not** need to be in the consuming app (`org-next`). Org-next only forwards the bearer token; it never signs or verifies that JWT. It should be set in:

- `apps/auth/.env.local`
- `../anedot_next/.env`

It should **not** be required in `apps/org-next/.env.local`. I should clean up `apps/auth/INTEGRATING.md` and the `apps/org-next/.env.template` comment to say that clearly.

input.plan:
# Fix `invalid_auth_state` for org-next local sign-in

## Diagnosis

`invalid_auth_state` happens before sign-in. It is raised by the auth app when this check fails in [`apps/auth/src/features/auth/server-functions.ts`](apps/auth/src/features/auth/server-functions.ts):

```ts
const verified = verifyAuthState(app, data.auth_state);
if (!verified.ok) {
  throw redirect({ to: "/error", search: { reason: "invalid_auth_state" } });
}
```

The signature is created by org-next with [`apps/org-next/src/server/auth-app-url.server.ts`](apps/org-next/src/server/auth-app-url.server.ts), which passes `env.SESSION_SECRET` into `buildLoginUrl`:

```ts
return buildLoginUrl(env.VITE_AUTH_APP_URL, env.SESSION_SECRET, returnTo);
```

The auth app verifies the same token using the `sessionSecretEnv` value from `AUTH_APPS`, resolved in [`apps/auth/src/lib/app-integration.ts`](apps/auth/src/lib/app-integration.ts). Your current local envs have `apps/org-next/.env.local` `SESSION_SECRET` and `apps/auth/.env.local` `ORG_NEXT_SESSION_SECRET` set to different values, so HMAC verification fails and auth redirects to `/error?reason=invalid_auth_state`.

## Plan

1. In [`apps/auth/.env.local`](apps/auth/.env.local), set `ORG_NEXT_SESSION_SECRET` to exactly the same value as [`apps/org-next/.env.local`](apps/org-next/.env.local) `SESSION_SECRET`.

2. In [`apps/org-next/.env.local`](apps/org-next/.env.local), remove `AUTH_SESSION_SECRET`; org-next does not read it and it can be confused with `SESSION_SECRET`.

3. Keep `AUTH_SESSION_SECRET` only in [`apps/auth/.env.local`](apps/auth/.env.local). It is auth-app-private and should not match org-next's `SESSION_SECRET` unless by accident.

4. Fix the next local-dev auth concern while we are there: in [`../anedot_next/.env`](../anedot_next/.env), replace the placeholder `LOCAL_DEV_JWT_SECRET` with the same value used by [`apps/auth/.env.local`](apps/auth/.env.local) `LOCAL_DEV_JWT_SECRET`. This does not cause `invalid_auth_state`, but it will cause Rails 401s after sign-in if left mismatched.

5. Optionally remove the legacy `VITE_AUTH_MOCK_MODE=true` from [`apps/auth/.env.local`](apps/auth/.env.local) since `ANEDOT_LOCAL_DEV=true` is now the canonical flag.

6. Restart both dev servers after changing env files:

```bash
pnpm --filter org-next dev
pnpm --filter auth dev
```

Restart `anedot_next` too if the Rails secret is updated.

## Expected Result

After the two app secrets match, org-next will sign `auth_state` with the same secret the auth app uses to verify it. The redirect should go from org-next `/login` to auth `/sign-in` instead of `/error?reason=invalid_auth_state`.

<timestamp>Wednesday, May 6, 2026, 9:12 AM (UTC-4)</timestamp>
<user_query>
I'm confused, explain what these three variables are each for in the auth app:
ORG_NEXT_SESSION_SECRET="7cf638a3ef2cdf5394fba5b5b1f7789ae369d50e4e9c9cf3283998887303277e"
AUTH_SESSION_SECRET="7cf638a3ef2cdf5394fba5b5b1f7789ae369d50e4e9c9cf3283998887303277e"
LOCAL_DEV_JWT_SECRET="933cd18664749b928c83508418c587ac902d4131751c4bf3d2f583fcf84fee8e"
</user_query>

These are three different trust boundaries. Two of yours currently happen to have the same value, but they do not mean the same thing.

`ORG_NEXT_SESSION_SECRET`
This is the shared secret between **auth** and **org-next** for the app-mode integration. In `AUTH_APPS`, auth has:

```env
AUTH_APPS={"org-next":{"sessionSecretEnv":"ORG_NEXT_SESSION_SECRET", ...}}
```

Auth uses `ORG_NEXT_SESSION_SECRET` to:

- verify the `auth_state` that org-next signed before redirecting to `/authorize`
- write TanStack signed cookies (`authToken`, `refreshToken`, `idToken`) that org-next can later read

This value **must match** `apps/org-next/.env.local`:

```env
SESSION_SECRET=...
```

`AUTH_SESSION_SECRET`
This is private to the **auth app itself**. Auth uses it for its own internal sessions, like:

- temporary transaction state while a sign-in flow is in progress
- challenge/session state
- auth app’s own internal cookies
- fallback secret for auth-owned cookie/bootstrap helpers

This value does **not** need to match org-next. It can be different. It is named similarly, which is confusing, but it is not the org-next cookie secret.

`LOCAL_DEV_JWT_SECRET`
This is shared between **auth** and **anedot_next Rails** only. In local dev, auth signs a fake-but-real-shaped JWT access token with this secret. Rails verifies that JWT with the same secret when `ANEDOT_LOCAL_DEV=true`.

Auth uses it to sign:

```text
Authorization bearer JWT
```

Rails uses it to verify:

```text
Authorization: Bearer <jwt>
```

Org-next does **not** need this secret. Org-next just forwards the token to Rails.

So the relationships are:

```text
ORG_NEXT_SESSION_SECRET
auth <-> org-next
must equal org-next SESSION_SECRET

AUTH_SESSION_SECRET
auth only
can be unique to auth

LOCAL_DEV_JWT_SECRET
auth <-> anedot_next
must equal anedot_next LOCAL_DEV_JWT_SECRET
```

For your example:

```env
ORG_NEXT_SESSION_SECRET="7cf..."
AUTH_SESSION_SECRET="7cf..."
LOCAL_DEV_JWT_SECRET="933..."
```

This is valid, but I would recommend changing `AUTH_SESSION_SECRET` to its own generated value so it’s obvious it is not coupled to org-next. The important part is:

- `ORG_NEXT_SESSION_SECRET` equals `apps/org-next` `SESSION_SECRET`
- `LOCAL_DEV_JWT_SECRET` equals `../anedot_next` `LOCAL_DEV_JWT_SECRET`
- `AUTH_SESSION_SECRET` just needs to be 32+ chars and private to auth.
